2.title: Accent Marks on Words
2.body: Accent marks in Italian indicate stress on the final vowel and distinguish words that would otherwise be spelled identically.
2.tip: Remember that words ending with an accented vowel have stress on that final syllable.

2.title: Qualcosa da + Infinitive
2.body: Use 'qualcosa da' + infinitive to express 'something to' do, describing an action needed or available.
2.tip: Think 'something to' + verb - the infinitive stays in its base form after 'da'.

2.title: Formal Address with Lei
2.body: The formal pronoun 'Lei' (you) is capitalized in writing to show respect, and it uses third-person singular verb conjugations.
2.tip: Think of capitalizing 'Lei' like capitalizing a title (e.g., Mr., Mrs.) to show respect.

2.title: Using Ecco with Pronouns
2.body: Ecco is a pointing word that can be combined with direct object pronouns to form single words like eccolo (here he/it is) or eccoli (here they are).

examples.en: Here's the book. → Here it is!
Here are the boys. → Here they are!

2.tip: Think of ecco + pronoun as 'here it/they is/are' - the pronoun attaches directly to ecco without spaces.

3.title: Definite Articles with Singular Nouns
3.body: Use 'il' before most masculine singular nouns, 'lo' before masculine nouns starting with z, s+consonant, or certain consonant clusters, and 'l'' before masculine nouns starting with a vowel.
3.tip: Remember: il = normal masculine, lo = special masculine (z, s+consonant), l' = vowel masculine

3.title: Italian Tens + Uno/Otto
3.body: When combining tens (venti, trenta, etc.) with 'uno' or 'otto', drop the final vowel of the tens numeral.
3.tip: Think: 'uno' and 'otto' are special - they make the tens lose their final vowel.

4.title: Telling Time in Italian
4.body: Use 'è' for singular hours (1:00) and 'sono le' for plural hours (2:00-12:00). Minutes after the hour use 'e', minutes before use 'meno'.

examples.it: Sono le sei meno un quarto

4.tip: Think: 'è' for one o'clock only, 'sono le' for all other hours.

5.title: Direct Object Pronouns with Infinitives
5.body: When an infinitive follows a conjugated verb, direct object pronouns attach to the infinitive, which then drops its final -e.

examples.it: Faccio portare le valigie → Faccio portarle.
examples.en: I have the suitcases brought → I have them brought.

examples.it: Voglio vedere il film → Voglio vederlo.
examples.en: I want to see the film → I want to see it.

5.tip: Think: 'Attach to the infinitive, drop the -e' when verbs are chained together.

6.title: Placement of 'anche'
6.body: The word 'anche' (also/too) normally comes immediately before the word or phrase it refers to.

examples.en: I would also like another blanket.
I would also like another blanket.

6.tip: Think of 'anche' as pointing directly at what comes next - it modifies whatever follows it.

6.title: Fare + Infinitive Construction
6.body: Use fare + infinitive to express having something done or having someone do something. The final -e of fare often drops in common constructions.

examples.en: I would like to have three dresses ironed.
I need to have my shoes shined.

6.tip: Think 'make/do' + action = have something done. Remember: 'far' not 'fare' before infinitives.

6.title: Partitive Articles with Preposition 'di'
6.body: When the preposition 'di' combines with definite articles, it forms partitive articles meaning 'some' or 'any' with uncountable nouns.
6.tip: Remember: di + il = del, di + lo = dello, di + la = della, di + i = dei, di + gli = degli, di + le = delle

6.title: Partitive Articles (some/any)
6.body: Use partitive articles to express 'some' or 'any' before nouns. The form changes based on gender, number, and the starting letter of the following word.

examples.en: Do you have any Italian friends?

6.tip: Think of partitive articles as 'di + definite article' - del = di + il, dei = di + i, etc.

6.title: Three Uses of 'le'
6.body: 'Le' has three uses: feminine plural article (le valigie), feminine plural direct object pronoun (le metto), and formal singular indirect object pronoun (Le occorre).
6.tip: Remember: article 'le' = the, object 'le' = them, formal 'Le' = to you (capitalized for formal address).

6.title: Identification Sentences with 'È'
6.body: Use 'è' (is) for identification sentences. Add 'no?' for verification questions, and 'non' for negative statements.
6.tip: Think of 'è' as the Italian 'is' for identifying people/things. Add 'no?' to check, 'non' to deny.

7.title: Forms of Address with Titles
7.body: Use 'il signor' for a man, 'la signora' for a married or older woman, and 'la signorina' for a young or unmarried woman, followed by the person's last name.
7.tip: Remember: 'signor' drops the final 'e' before a consonant, unlike 'signore' (sir).

7.title: Infinitive -e Dropping
7.body: When attaching object pronouns to infinitives, drop the final -e from the -re ending.
7.tip: Pronoun attachment = lose the -e: infinitivere → infinitiver + pronoun

7.title: Verb Forms for 'We', 'You' (plural), 'They'
7.body: The verb endings -iamo, -ate, and -ano correspond to the subjects 'we', 'you' (plural), and 'they' for regular -are verbs like 'portare'.
7.tip: Remember: 'We' ends with -iamo, 'you all' with -ate, and 'they' with -ano.

8.title: Preposition-Article Contractions
8.body: The prepositions a, di, da, in, and su combine with definite articles to form single contracted words.
8.tip: Learn the contraction patterns as single units rather than trying to combine them each time.

8.title: Formal Commands: Lei vs Loro
8.body: Formal singular (Lei) commands drop the -no from the formal plural (Loro) form, which is derived from the present tense loro form by changing -ano to -ino or -ono to -ano.
8.tip: Think: Lei = Loro minus -no. Singular formal drops the 'no' from plural formal.

8.title: C'è vs. Ci sono with Ne
8.body: Use 'c'è' for singular and 'ci sono' for plural. When replacing a noun with 'ne', 'ci' becomes 'ce' before 'ne'.

examples.it: Ce ne sono tanti qui vicino.

8.title: Direct Object Pronouns
8.body: Direct object pronouns replace nouns and agree in gender and number with what they replace: lo (masc. sing.), la (fem. sing.), li (masc. pl.), le (fem. pl.).
8.tip: Think LO for him/it (masc.), LA for her/it (fem.), add -I for masculine plural, -E for feminine plural.

9.title: Voi Command Form
9.body: The voi command form is identical to the present indicative voi form, except for four irregular verbs: essere, avere, sapere, and volere.
9.tip: Remember the four irregulars with the acronym SAVS: S(ere), A(vere), V(olere), S(apere).
9.source_label: Grammar Note 6: The voi request form

9.title: Reflexive Verbs with Lei Form
9.body: Reflexive infinitives ending in -si use 'si' for the polite Lei form, not the standard reflexive pronoun 'si'.
9.tip: For Lei form, just add 'si' before the conjugated verb - same as infinitive ending!

9.title: Formal Imperative with Lei
9.body: Use the third person singular subjunctive (congiuntivo) for formal commands when addressing someone with 'Lei'.

examples.it: Se può darmi la somma giusta...
examples.en: If you could give me the exact amount...

examples.it: Mi dia il Suo nome, prego.

9.tip: Think 'he/she' form for polite 'you' commands: instead of 'dammi' (informal), use 'mi dia' (formal).

9.title: da + Infinitive for 'to'
9.body: Use 'da' + infinitive to express 'to' or 'for' when describing something that needs to be done.

examples.en: How long is there to wait?

9.tip: Think of 'da' as 'to be done' - something da fare = something to be done.

10.title: Short Answers with 'For'
10.body: Use 'for' + a time period to give a short answer about duration, often in response to 'How long...?' questions.

examples.en: I have been here for two weeks.
I have worked here for a year.

10.tip: Think 'da' in Italian for 'for' when talking about a duration that started in the past and continues.

10.title: Word Order with 'qui/lì' and Names
10.body: In statements, names can appear before or after 'qui/lì', but in yes-no questions they typically come at the end.
10.tip: Think: statements = flexible order, questions = name last.

10.title: Placement of Names with 'c'è'
10.body: In questions and affirmative statements with 'c'è' (there is) or 'non c'è' (there isn't), names normally follow the verb phrase.
10.tip: Think: 'Is there Bianca?' not 'Bianca is there?' for the normal word order.

11.title: Formal 'You' in Questions
11.body: In formal questions, the pronoun 'Lei' can be placed at the end or directly after the verb.
11.tip: Think of 'Lei' as flexible; it can follow the verb or the question word.

11.title: Required Words in Italian
11.body: Some words, like subject pronouns or articles, are grammatically required in Italian sentences even when they are omitted in English.
11.tip: If an English sentence feels 'bare,' check if Italian needs a pronoun or article to be grammatically complete.

11.title: Question Tags with 'no?' and 'vero?'
11.body: Turn any statement into a question by adding 'no?' or 'vero?' at the end.

examples.it: Lei è la signora Contini, no?
Lei è la signora Contini, vero?
examples.en: You are Mrs. Contini, aren't you?

11.tip: Think of 'no?' as 'right?' and 'vero?' as 'true?' - both work like English question tags.

11.title: Quale + è contraction
11.body: The word 'quale' (which) contracts to 'qual' when followed by the verb 'è' (is).
11.tip: Think of it like 'qual' + 'è' = 'qual'è' (always with apostrophe)

11.title: Definite Articles with Masculine Nouns
11.body: Use 'il' for most masculine singular nouns, but 'lo' before z, s+consonant, or vowel (which becomes l').
11.tip: Remember ZSS: Z, S+consonant, and vowel Sounds get 'lo' (or l').

11.title: Preposition 'da' with Time Expressions
11.body: Use 'da' to indicate the starting point of an action that continues into the present, equivalent to 'since' or 'for' in English.

examples.it: Lavoro in questa sezione da quattro anni.
examples.en: I have worked in this section for four years.

examples.it: Lei lavora all'Ambasciata da molto tempo.
examples.en: She has worked at the Embassy for a long time.

11.tip: Think of 'da' as pointing back to when something started - it's the starting point marker for ongoing actions.

11.title: Gender Agreement in Adjectives
11.body: Italian adjectives must match the gender of the person they describe, changing their ending accordingly.
11.tip: Remember: -o endings are typically masculine, -a endings are typically feminine.

12.title: Articles with Nouns Starting with Z or S+Consonant
12.body: Use 'uno' for masculine nouns beginning with Z or S followed by a consonant, instead of 'un'.
12.tip: Remember Z and S+consonant need 'uno' - think 'Zero Special' cases.

12.title: Future Tense for Probability
12.body: The future tense can express present probability or conjecture, not just future actions.

examples.en: They probably go to school, then.

12.tip: Think 'future form, present guess' - when you're unsure about something happening now.

12.title: Prepositions with Origin
12.body: Use 'da' with verbs of movement like 'venire' to indicate coming from a place, and 'di' with 'essere' to indicate origin or belonging.
12.tip: Think: 'venire DA' = coming FROM, 'essere DI' = belonging TO a place.

12.title: Common Italian Verb Expressions
12.body: Italian uses specific verb + preposition combinations to express common actions. These fixed expressions must be memorized as units.
12.tip: Think of these as 'verb packages' - learn the whole expression (verb + preposition) together, not just the verb alone.

12.title: Expressing Age with Avere
12.body: In Italian, age is expressed using the verb avere (to have) instead of essere (to be).

examples.en: How old are you? (lit. How many years do you have?)
Marco is 25 years old. (lit. Marco has 25 years.)

12.tip: Think 'I have years' instead of 'I am years old' - you possess your age in Italian.

12.title: Asking 'How long' with 'essere da'
12.body: To ask how long someone has been in a place, use 'essere' + 'da' + time period. The verb agrees with the subject.

examples.it: Da quanto tempo sei a Roma?
examples.en: How long have you been in Rome?

examples.it: Sono a Milano da tre giorni.
examples.en: I have been in Milan for three days.

12.tip: Think 'to be from' a point in time: 'essere da' = 'to be since/for'.

12.title: Preposition-Article Contractions
12.body: The preposition 'da' contracts with definite articles to form single words, like 'da + la = dalla'. The contraction 'dall'' is used before vowels.
12.tip: Think of 'da' merging with the article; 'dall'' is the vowel-friendly version of 'dallo' and 'dalla'.

12.title: Future Tense Formation
12.body: Form the future tense by removing the final -e from the infinitive and adding future endings: -ò, -à, -emo, -ete, -anno. For -are verbs, change the -a to -e first.
12.tip: Drop the -e, add endings. Remember: -are verbs become -ere verbs first (parlare → parler-).

12.title: Present Subjunctive Formation
12.body: The present subjunctive is formed by taking the present indicative 'loro' form, dropping '-no', and adding specific endings: -i, -i, -a, -iamo, -iate, -ano.
12.tip: Start with 'loro' form (parlano), drop '-no' (parla-), add subjunctive endings: -i, -i, -a, -iamo, -iate, -ano.

13.title: Adjective Order for Colors
13.body: When describing shades of colors, the color name comes first, followed by the shade adjective (like chiaro or scuro).
13.tip: Think 'color + shade' in Italian, opposite of English order.

13.title: Definite Articles with Titles
13.body: The definite article (il, la, etc.) always precedes ranks, titles, and forms of address like Signor, Signora, and Signorina.
13.tip: Think: 'the' + title, just like 'the Captain' in English.

13.title: Adjective Position with Colors
13.body: Most color adjectives follow the noun, but compound color adjectives (like 'grigio scuro') come before the noun.
13.tip: Simple colors go after, compound colors (two words) go before the noun.

13.title: Impersonal Si Construction
13.body: Use 'si' + verb to express general statements about what people do. The verb agrees with the subject that follows it.
13.tip: Think of 'si' as 'one' or 'people' - it makes statements general rather than about specific people.

13.title: Dovere + Verb
13.body: Use 'dovere' + infinitive verb to express obligation or necessity. The modal verb 'dovere' conjugates while the main verb stays in infinitive form.
13.tip: Think 'must do' - 'dovere' changes with the subject, but the action verb stays in its 'to-' form.

13.title: Formal vs Informal Questions with Avere
13.body: Use 'Ha' for formal 'you' (Lei) and 'Avete' for plural 'you' (voi) when asking questions with avere.
13.tip: Ha for one person (formal), Avete for multiple people (informal or formal plural).

13.title: Asking 'Do you have...?' with 'avere'
13.body: Use the verb 'avere' (to have) to ask and answer about possession. The question form is 'Hai...?' for 'you' (informal).
13.tip: Remember 'hai' for 'you have' - it's the key to asking about what someone possesses.

13.title: Avoiding Possessive Forms
13.body: Italian often avoids possessive adjectives when an indirect object pronoun already indicates the possessor.

examples.en: I take her measurements. (Literally: I take the measurements to her)
I wash my hair. (Literally: I wash the hair to myself)

13.tip: Think 'to whom' rather than 'whose' - use indirect pronouns (mi, ti, le, gli, ci, vi) instead of possessive adjectives.

13.title: Verb piacere construction
13.body: Piacere means 'to be pleasing' and uses indirect object pronouns to indicate who likes something. The verb agrees with the thing being liked, not the person.

examples.en: I like the jacket. (The jacket is pleasing to me)
I like the pants. (The pants are pleasing to me)

13.tip: Think 'it pleases me' not 'I like it' - the verb matches the thing liked (singular/plural).

13.title: Identification Sentences with 'È'
13.body: Use 'è' (is) to identify objects, with optional question tags or negation. Questions can be formed by intonation or adding 'che cos'è'.
13.tip: Think of 'è' as the Italian 'is' for identification - same structure for statements, negatives, and questions.

14.title: Asking and Answering 'Where in (city)?'
14.body: Use 'in' plus the city name to ask where someone lives within a city. The preposition 'in' does not contract with articles for city names.

examples.en: Where do you live in Rome?

14.tip: Think 'in' for being 'in' a city, just like in English.

14.title: Number Formation from 21 Onwards
14.body: For numbers 21-99, the tens and units can be written as separate words or combined into one, with the final vowel of the tens digit often dropped before 'uno' or 'otto'.
14.tip: Think 'BUMP' for the numbers where the pattern applies: 1, 8, and their multiples in the ones place trigger the vowel drop.

14.title: Agreement with Past Participles
14.body: When using avere as the auxiliary verb, the past participle must agree in gender and number with any preceding direct object pronouns (lo, la, li, le).

examples.it: Ho visto la ragazza → L'ho vista
examples.en: I saw the girl → I saw her

examples.it: Ho comprato i libri → Li ho comprati
examples.en: I bought the books → I bought them

14.tip: Remember: With avere, agreement happens only when the object pronoun comes BEFORE the verb.

14.title: Verb Placement with Prepositions
14.body: Some verbs require prepositions before infinitives, while others don't. 'Aspettare' can follow directly or with 'a' depending on the preceding verb.
14.tip: Think: 'go to wait' needs 'a' like English, but simple 'wait' stands alone.

14.title: Direct Object Pronouns with Passato Prossimo
14.body: When using direct object pronouns with passato prossimo, the past participle must agree in gender and number with the pronoun it refers to.

examples.en: I brought you these shoes (agrees with 'scarpe')

14.tip: Match the ending of the past participle to the pronoun: -o for masculine singular, -i for masculine plural, -a for feminine singular, -e for feminine plural.

15.title: Plural Nouns with Singular Meaning
15.body: Some Italian nouns are grammatically plural but refer to singular concepts, requiring plural agreement for adjectives and pronouns.
15.tip: Remember: plural form doesn't always mean plural meaning - check agreement patterns.

15.title: Prepositions with Infinitives
15.body: Use 'per' before an infinitive when it means 'in order to' to express purpose.

examples.en: I study in order to learn Italian.

examples.it: Vado al mercato per comprare frutta.
examples.en: I go to the market to buy fruit.

15.tip: Think 'per' = 'for the purpose of' when followed by a verb.

15.title: Agreement with 'fatto'
15.body: The past participle 'fatto' must agree in gender and number with the subject of the verb 'essere'.

examples.en: How is the cake (feminine singular) made?
How are the cookies (masculine plural) made?

15.tip: Think: 'fatto' changes like an adjective to match what it describes.

15.title: Using 'prendere' for ordering
15.body: Use the verb 'prendere' to indicate what you are going to order or have to eat or drink.

examples.en: The lady is going to have a salad.

15.tip: Think: 'prendere' = to take/order. It's the standard verb for choosing food or drink.

15.title: Object Pronoun Redundancy for Emphasis
15.body: When a direct object noun is placed before the verb for emphasis, you must also use a corresponding direct object pronoun with the verb.

examples.it: Il biglietto lo prende dal tabaccaio.
examples.en: The ticket, he buys it at the tobacco shop.

15.tip: Think 'noun + pronoun' when emphasizing the object upfront - both are required.

15.title: Pronoun Combination Changes
15.body: The pronouns mi, si, ci, vi, gli change to me, se, ce, ve, glie when combined with ne, lo, la, li, le.

examples.en: Bring us three servings of it.

15.tip: Think 'me-se-ce-ve-glie' as the special forms before other pronouns like 'ne' or 'lo'.

15.title: Definite Article LO
15.body: Use 'lo' (not 'il') before masculine singular nouns starting with 's' + consonant or the 'sc' sound before 'e' or 'i'.
15.tip: Think 'lo' for 's' + consonant or 'sc' sound (like 'chef') before e/i.

15.title: Combined Pronouns: Glie + Direct Object
15.body: When combining indirect object pronouns (gli/le) with direct object pronouns (lo/la/li/le/ne), 'glie' is used and written as one word with the direct object pronoun.

examples.en: Giovanni will give them to him/her/you/them tomorrow.
I'll give them to you tomorrow.

15.tip: Think 'glie' + direct object = one word: glielo, gliela, glieli, gliele, gliene

15.title: Negation with Non...
15.body: In Italian, 'non' is placed before the verb to create negation, often combined with other negative words like 'mai' (never) or 'niente' (nothing).
15.tip: Think of 'non' as the anchor—it always comes before the verb, and other negative words follow naturally.

15.title: Placement of 'c'è' and 'lì'
15.body: In Italian location sentences, 'c'è' (there is) can come before or after the location word 'lì' (there), with the same meaning.
15.tip: Think of it as flexible word order: both 'There is a sofa there' and 'There, there is a sofa' work in Italian.

16.title: Imperfect Tense of 'Essere'
16.body: The verb 'essere' (to be) has irregular forms in the imperfect tense, which describes ongoing or habitual past actions.
16.tip: All imperfect forms start with 'er-' except 'era' (he/she/it was) which is shorter.

16.title: Placement of 'Non' in Health Questions
16.body: In questions about health using 'stare', 'non' can be placed at the beginning to ask if someone is not well, or after the verb in statements.
16.tip: Think: 'Non' first for questioning health, after verb for stating poor health.

17.title: Preposition 'da' in Room Descriptions
17.body: Use 'da' to indicate the purpose or function of a room, connecting the room type with its use.
17.tip: Think of 'da' as meaning 'for' when describing what a room is for.

17.title: Ce l'ho construction
17.body: In spoken Italian, 'ce' is placed before 'l'' with forms of 'avere' to avoid awkward consonant clusters and clarify meaning.
17.tip: Think 'ce' as a buffer before 'l' + avere forms - it makes pronunciation smoother and prevents confusion.

17.title: Titles with/without Articles
17.body: Use titles without articles when directly addressing someone, but with articles when referring to them in third person.
17.tip: Direct address = no article; talking about = article + title
17.source_label: TITLES IN DIRECT ADDRESS OR NOT

18.title: Possessives with Kinship Terms
18.body: Most singular possessives require the definite article (il/la), but kinship terms like padre, madre, figlio, figlia do not take articles.
18.tip: Think: family members are close, so no article needed between possessive and kinship term.

18.title: Location Sentences with C'è and È
18.body: Use 'c'è' for 'there is' with indefinite nouns and 'è' for 'is' with definite nouns or proper names.
18.tip: C'è = there is (something indefinite); È = is (someone/something specific)

19.title: Studio vs. Ufficio
19.body: Use 'studio' for professional offices like doctors or lawyers, not 'ufficio'.
19.tip: Think 'studio' for professionals who study their field.

19.title: Piacere Construction
19.body: Piacere means 'to be pleasing' and requires indirect object pronouns. The thing liked is the subject, and the person liking is the indirect object.

examples.en: Naples is pleasing to me (I like Naples).
Is pizza pleasing to you? (Do you like pizza?)

19.tip: Think 'it pleases me' instead of 'I like it' - the thing you like does the action of pleasing.

19.title: Ora, Tempo, Volta - Different 'Times'
19.body: Ora means clock time, tempo means duration, and volta means occurrence. They are not interchangeable despite all translating to 'time' in English.

examples.en: How much time do we have?

19.tip: Ora = hour (clock), Tempo = temporal (duration), Volta = volte (times/count)

19.title: Sapere vs Potere
19.body: Use 'sapere' for knowing how to do something (skill/ability), and 'potere' for being able to do something (permission/circumstance).

examples.en: He doesn't know how to speak Italian.

examples.it: Non può parlare; ha la laringite.
examples.en: He can't speak; he has laryngitis.

19.source_label: Lesson XIX Grammar Explanation: Sapere vs Potere

20.title: Verbs with Pronoun 'ci'
20.body: The pronoun 'ci' can attach to verbs like 'prendere' to mean 'for ourselves' or 'for us'.

examples.it: Andiamo al bar a prenderci qualcosa?
examples.en: Shall we go to the bar to get something for ourselves?

20.tip: Think of 'ci' as adding 'for us' to the verb's meaning.

20.title: Definite Articles for Categories
20.body: Use the definite article (il, la, etc.) when referring to categories like public places, transportation, instruments, or illnesses, not just specific items.

examples.it: Andiamo al bar o al ristorante?
examples.en: Shall we go to a café or a restaurant?

examples.it: Sono venuto in bicicletta; non ho preso il taxi.
examples.en: I came by bicycle; I didn't take a taxi.

20.tip: Think of it as 'the' for whole categories (like 'the piano' meaning pianos in general), not just one specific thing.

20.title: Andare + a + Infinitive
20.body: The verb 'andare' requires the preposition 'a' before a following infinitive verb.

examples.en: Let's go get ourselves a coffee.

20.tip: Think 'andare a' as a fixed phrase meaning 'to go to do something' - similar to English structure.

20.title: Subject Pronouns for Emphasis
20.body: Subject pronouns are required in Italian for emphasis and often follow the verb in short phrases.
20.tip: Think: 'I'll do it!' → 'Lo faccio io!' - the pronoun adds emphasis.

20.title: Imperative Irregularities
20.body: Some Italian verbs have irregular imperative forms, especially in the 'tu' form, which don't follow regular conjugation patterns.
20.tip: Remember these common irregulars: di' (dire), sii (essere), abbi (avere), sappi (sapere), vogli (volere).

20.title: Forming the 'tu' Imperative
20.body: For -are verbs, the 'tu' imperative is identical to the 'lei' present indicative form. For -ere and -ire verbs, it is identical to the 'tu' present indicative form.
20.tip: For -are verbs, think 'lei' form; for others, think 'tu' present.

20.title: Invitation Formulas with 'Noi'
20.body: Use present tense 'noi' form, 'Perché non + noi form', or 'vogliamo + infinitive' to make suggestions or invitations.

examples.en: Shall we go to the bar?

examples.it: Perché non ci diamo del tu?
examples.en: Why don't we use the informal 'tu'?

20.tip: Three ways to suggest: 1) Simple 'noi' verb, 2) 'Why not + noi', 3) 'We want + infinitive'.

20.title: Fra vs In for Time Expressions
20.body: Use 'fra' followed by a time period to mean '... from now', while 'in' indicates duration or completion within that time.

examples.en: I'll have dinner in two hours (from now).
I'll finish dinner in two hours (duration).

20.tip: Think of 'fra' as 'from now' and 'in' as 'within' when talking about time periods.

20.title: Expressing Likes with Verbs
20.body: Use 'piacere' + infinitive to say you like doing something. The indirect object pronoun (mi, ti, gli, etc.) comes before 'piace' to indicate who likes the action.
20.tip: Think: 'To me it pleases to work' = 'I like to work'. The person is the indirect object, not the subject.

21.title: Diminutive Suffixes
21.body: The suffix '-etta' is added to nouns to create a diminutive form, indicating something is small or expressing affection.
21.tip: Think of '-etta' as the Italian '-ette' in 'kitchenette'—it makes things smaller.

21.title: Adjective Agreement with Gender
21.body: Some adjectives like 'grande' have the same form for masculine and feminine, while others like 'questo/questa' change endings based on the noun's gender.

examples.it: un grande uomo / una grande donna
examples.en: a great man / a great woman

21.tip: Remember: 'grande' is gender-neutral, but 'questo' changes to 'questa' for feminine nouns.

21.title: Using 'ne' with Verbs
21.body: The particle 'ne' replaces a partitive phrase (di + noun) and is placed before the conjugated verb, except with the imperative and infinitive.
21.tip: Think of 'ne' as 'of it/them' and attach it to the verb's end in commands or infinitives.

21.title: Reflexive Verb Pronouns
21.body: Reflexive verbs ending in -si require matching reflexive pronouns before the verb: mi, ti, si, ci, vi, si.
21.tip: Match the pronoun to the subject: io → mi, tu → ti, lui/lei → si, noi → ci, voi → vi, loro → si.

21.title: Reflexive Verbs in Passato Prossimo
21.body: Reflexive verbs use essere as auxiliary in passato prossimo, and the past participle agrees in gender and number with the subject.

examples.en: We met each other last Monday.

21.tip: With reflexive verbs, think 'essere + agreement' - the past participle changes like an adjective.
21.source_label: Grammar Note: Reflexive Verbs and Past Tense Usage

21.title: Present Tense with 'È la prima volta che'
21.body: Use the present tense after 'È la prima volta che' to express something happening for the first time right now.

examples.it: È la prima volta che viene in Italia?
examples.en: Is it the first time you're coming to Italy?

examples.it: È la seconda volta che mi trovo in questa situazione.
examples.en: It's the second time I find myself in this situation.

21.tip: Think: 'first time NOW' = present tense, even if in English we might use present perfect.

21.title: Essere di vs. Venire da
21.body: Use 'essere di' to indicate origin (born/raised in a city/town). Use 'venire da' to indicate coming from a place, often temporarily.
21.tip: 'Essere di' is for identity (like 'I am a Milanese'), 'venire da' is for movement (like 'I come from there').

21.title: Definite Articles with Languages
21.body: Use l' before language names like italiano, francese, spagnolo, except after parlare, in, or di where the article is optional or omitted.
21.tip: Think: 'l'italiano' is standard, but drop it after parlare, in, di.

21.title: Imperfetto Tense Formation
21.body: The imperfetto past tense is formed by replacing the -re of the infinitive with endings containing -v- (except for essere). It describes ongoing or habitual past actions.

examples.en: I liked the city (during a period)

21.tip: Look for the -v- in the ending: parlare → parlavo, parlavi, parlava, etc.

21.title: Present Tense Meanings
21.body: The Italian present tense describes actions happening now, habitual actions, or future plans, not just current moments.

examples.en: He is going to call tomorrow.

21.tip: Think 'NOW, ALWAYS, or SOON' - Italian present covers all three time frames.

21.title: Irregular Imperfetto Verbs
21.body: Three common verbs have irregular stems in the imperfetto tense: essere (er-), dire (dicev-), and fare (facev-).

examples.it: io ero, tu eri, lui/lei era
examples.en: I was, you were, he/she was

examples.it: io dicevo, tu dicevi, lui/lei diceva
examples.en: I said, you said, he/she said

examples.it: io facevo, tu facevi, lui/lei faceva
examples.en: I did/made, you did/made, he/she did/made

21.tip: Remember the 'E-D-F' trio: Essere, Dire, Fare - the only verbs with irregular imperfetto stems.

21.title: Prepositions with Places: A, In, Di
21.body: Use 'di' for origin (È di Roma), 'a' for cities/small islands (a Roma, ad Haiti), and 'in' for countries/large islands (in Italia, in Sicilia).

examples.it: Vivo a Milano, ma sono di Napoli.
examples.en: I live in Milan, but I am from Naples.

examples.it: Andiamo in Francia e in Corsica.
examples.en: We are going to France and to Corsica.

21.tip: Cities get 'a', countries get 'in', origin gets 'di'.

22.title: C'è vs Ci sono
22.body: Use 'c'è' for singular and 'ci sono' for plural to express existence or presence of something.

examples.en: There is a book on the table.

examples.it: Ci sono due libri sul tavolo.
examples.en: There are two books on the table.

22.tip: Remember: C'è = singular (one thing), Ci sono = plural (multiple things). Add 'non' before for negatives.
22.source_label: C’è. There is. Ci sono. There are. Non c’è. There is not. Non ci sono. There are not.

22.title: Using 'Mi serve' for 'I need'
22.body: Use 'mi serve' to express needing a single item or service. It uses the verb 'servire' (to be necessary), which conjugates like the item needed is the subject.
22.tip: Think: 'A porter is necessary to me' = 'Mi serve un facchino.'

22.title: Ne pronoun usage
22.body: Ne replaces phrases introduced by di (of) and means 'of it' or 'of them'.

examples.it: Mi dia una dozzina di uova → Me ne dia una dozzina
examples.en: Give me a dozen eggs → Give me a dozen of them

examples.it: Mi dia un chilo di parmigiano → Me ne dia un chilo
examples.en: Give me a kilo of parmesan → Give me a kilo of it

22.tip: Think of 'ne' as 'of it/them' - it replaces 'di + noun' phrases.

22.title: Occorre vs. Occorrono
22.body: Use singular 'occorre' when referring to something needed in general or a singular item, and plural 'occorrono' when referring to multiple specific items.
22.tip: Match 'occorre/occorrono' to the number of items: singular item = occorre, multiple items = occorrono.

22.title: Asking and Telling Time
22.body: Use 'è l'una' for 1:00 and 'sono le' for all other hours. Minutes follow the hour without 'e' unless specified.

examples.en: What time is it? It's one o'clock.

examples.it: Che ore sono? Sono le tre e venti.
examples.en: What time is it? It's three twenty.

22.tip: Remember: 1 is singular (è l'una), all others are plural (sono le due/tre...).

22.title: Ne with Quantities
22.body: The particle 'ne' replaces direct object nouns in quantitative phrases when the noun isn't expressed. It usually precedes the verb but attaches to infinitives.

examples.en: I don't want to buy any (of them).

22.tip: Think of 'ne' as meaning 'of them' when replacing unexpressed nouns after quantities.

22.title: Noun Markers
22.body: Noun markers are words or phrases that come before nouns to indicate gender, number, or quantity.
22.tip: Think of noun markers as 'noun introducers' - they always come before the noun they describe.

22.title: Gender: Masculine vs Feminine
22.body: Italian nouns are either masculine or feminine. Use 'un/uno' before masculine nouns and 'una/un'' before feminine nouns.
22.tip: Think: 'un' for masculine, 'una' for feminine. Most male beings use 'un', female beings use 'una'.

22.title: Gender Agreement Patterns
22.body: Some Italian nouns have fixed gender (masculine or feminine), while others can be either depending on the person they refer to.
22.tip: Remember: nouns ending in -o are usually masculine, -a usually feminine, but -e endings can be either gender.

23.title: Preposition 'di' for Categories
23.body: Use the preposition 'di' to indicate a category or type of something, often translating to 'of' in English.
23.tip: Think 'di' = 'of' when describing what something is made of or its category.

23.title: Singular Nouns for Clothing Sets
23.body: In Italian, nouns for clothing items that consist of two parts, like pajamas, are singular.
23.tip: Think of the set as one unit, so it gets a singular article and verb.

23.title: Adjective Agreement with Nouns
23.body: Adjectives must match the gender (masculine/feminine) and number (singular/plural) of the nouns they describe.
23.tip: Change adjective endings like -o/-a/-i/-e to match the noun's gender and number.

23.title: Passato Prossimo Formation
23.body: The passato prossimo is formed with the present tense of avere or essere plus the past participle of the main verb.
23.tip: Think 'have/has + past participle' like in English, but remember some verbs use essere instead of avere.

23.title: Come: How vs. As
23.body: The word 'come' can mean 'how' as a question word, or 'as' when making a comparison or giving an example.
23.tip: Think: 'Come' asks 'how' and introduces 'as' in examples.

23.title: Invariable Adjective Forms
23.body: Some adjectives have identical forms for masculine/feminine and singular/plural, changing only the article.
23.tip: Look for adjectives ending in -e (singular) and -i (plural) - they don't change for gender.

23.title: Direct Object Pronouns with 'prendo'
23.body: Direct object pronouns come before the conjugated verb 'prendo' and must agree in gender and number with the noun they replace.
23.tip: Remember the pattern: pronoun (la/le/lo/li) + prendo. Match gender (la/lo for singular, le/li for plural) and number.

23.title: Demonstrative Adjectives: This/That
23.body: Use 'questo' for 'this' (near) and 'quello' for 'that' (far). They agree in gender and number with the noun they modify.
23.tip: Remember: 'questo' = 'this' (close), 'quello' = 'that' (far). Match the ending to the noun's gender and number.

23.title: Passato Prossimo Formation
23.body: Form the passato prossimo with present tense of avere or essere plus past participle. Most verbs use avere, but essere is for movement, state changes, and reflexive verbs.
23.tip: Use essere when the verb describes coming/going, becoming, or reflexive actions (like lavarsi). Otherwise use avere.

23.title: Definite Articles with Preferire/Piacere
23.body: After verbs preferire and piacere, use definite articles (il, lo, la, i, gli, le) before nouns, even when not translated in English.
23.tip: Think: 'prefer the X' or 'like the X' in Italian, even if you'd say just 'prefer X' in English.

23.title: Passato Prossimo with Avere vs. Essere
23.body: Use 'avere' for most verbs and 'essere' for verbs of movement, change, or reflexive verbs. With 'essere', the past participle must agree in gender and number with the subject.
23.tip: Remember: Avere = actions, Essere = movement/change/reflexive. With Essere, make the ending match (o/a/i/e).

23.title: Imperative Pronouns Placement
23.body: With affirmative tu, noi, voi imperatives, object pronouns attach to the verb. With lei/loro imperatives, pronouns precede the verb.
23.tip: Attach pronouns after for tu/noi/voi, but before for lei/loro commands.

23.title: Preposition 'da' for Location
23.body: The preposition 'da' can mean 'at the place of' when followed by a person or pronoun, indicating someone's home or location.

examples.it: Vengono a trascorrere il weekend da noi.
examples.en: They're coming to spend the weekend at our place.

23.tip: Think of 'da' + person = 'at their place' - similar to 'at my place' or 'at their house' in English.

23.title: Infinitive After Verbs
23.body: Many Italian verbs are followed directly by the infinitive without any preposition.
23.tip: Think of 'modal verbs' like want, can, must - they take the infinitive directly.

23.title: Gender of Geographical Names
23.body: Most geographical names ending in -a are feminine, others are masculine. Cities, islands, and some US states are feminine; oceans, seas, rivers, lakes, and most US states are masculine.
23.tip: Think -a = feminine, but remember the five masculine exceptions ending in -a: Canadà, Ghana, Guatemala, Nicaragua, Venezuela.

23.title: Place Replacer: Ci
23.body: Use 'ci' to replace a previously mentioned place, especially with movement verbs. Place it before conjugated verbs or attach it to infinitives (dropping the final -e).

examples.it: Quando vai a Roma? Ci vado domani.
examples.en: When are you going to Rome? I'm going there tomorrow.
Do you want to go there with me?

23.tip: Think of 'ci' as 'there' - it replaces places you've already mentioned.

23.title: Using 'tu' vs. 'Lei'
23.body: Use 'tu' for informal address and 'Lei' for formal. The verb must agree with the pronoun being used.

examples.en: Shall we use the informal 'tu' with each other?

examples.it: Mi dia del tu, per favore.
examples.en: Please use the informal 'tu' with me.

23.tip: Remember: 'Lei' is formal and uses third-person verbs (like 'she'), while 'tu' is informal and uses second-person verbs.

24.title: Diminutive suffixes -etto/-etta
24.body: Add -etto (masculine) or -etta (feminine) to a noun to indicate a smaller or cuter version.
24.tip: Think of -etto/-etta as the Italian '-let' or 'little'.

24.title: Troppo as 'Too'
24.body: When troppo comes before an adjective, it means 'too' rather than 'too much/many'.

24.title: Reflexive Verb + di
24.body: The reflexive verb ricordarsi requires the preposition 'di' before the thing being remembered.
24.tip: Think 'ricordarsi di' as a fixed pair - the verb always brings its friend 'di' along.

24.title: Fare + Infinitive Construction
24.body: The construction 'fare + infinitive' means 'to have something done.' When 'fare' is used in its infinitive form before another infinitive, it often drops its final -e.

examples.en: I would like to have the car cleaned.

24.tip: Think 'fare' becomes 'far' when followed by another verb in the infinitive to make the phrase flow better.

24.title: Definite Article with Languages
24.body: Use 'l'' before language names (l'italiano), but omit it after prepositions like 'in' or 'd'' (d'italiano).
24.tip: Think: Article before, no article after prepositions (in/di).

24.title: Conditional Tense -RE Endings
24.body: The conditional tense uses -RE endings to express polite requests or hypothetical situations in the present.
24.tip: Think of -RE as 'Respectful Expression' for polite requests.

24.title: Formal Imperative for -are Verbs
24.body: For formal commands with Lei (singular) and Loro (plural), regular -are verbs change their endings to -i and -ino respectively.
24.tip: Think: formal commands swap -are for -i (singular) and -ino (plural).
24.source_label: FORMAL REQUEST FORMS OF REGULAR -ARE VERBS

24.title: Telephone Greetings
24.body: Use 'pronto' to answer the phone in Italian, which literally means 'ready' but functions as 'hello'.
24.tip: Think of 'pronto' as signaling you are 'ready' to speak.

25.title: Ordering with 'I'll take' or 'I would like'
25.body: Use 'Prendo' (I'll take) or 'Vorrei' (I would like) to order food or drinks, followed by the item.

examples.en: I would like the menu, please.

25.tip: Prendo is direct; Vorrei is more polite, using the conditional mood.

25.title: Indefinite Articles: uno, un, una, un'
25.body: Use 'uno' before masculine nouns starting with z or s+consonant. Use 'un' for other masculine nouns. Use 'una' before feminine consonants and 'un'' before feminine vowels.

examples.en: a book, a house, a friend

25.tip: Think: 'uno' for tricky masculine starts (z, s+consonant), 'un'' for feminine vowels (with apostrophe).

25.title: Frequency with 'a'
25.body: Use the preposition 'a' + definite article to express how often something happens per time unit.

25.title: Irregular Future of Vedere
25.body: The verb vedere has an irregular future stem: vedr- instead of the expected ved- + endings.

examples.en: I will see the film tomorrow.

25.tip: Remember: vedere → vedr- + future endings (ò, ai, à, emo, ete, anno).

25.title: Time Period + che + essere
25.body: Use a time expression followed by che + essere to specify or emphasize a particular day/date within that time period.

examples.it: ieri che era giorno di festa

25.tip: Think: 'Time period + that it is/was + specific day' - like adding a calendar note to a time word.

25.title: Reflexive Verb Agreement
25.body: The reflexive pronoun 'si' in 'mettersi d'accordo' must agree with the subject of the verb.

examples.it: Io mi metterò d'accordo con lui.
examples.en: I will reach an agreement with him.

examples.it: Loro non si metteranno mai d'accordo.
examples.en: They will never reach an agreement.

25.tip: Match the reflexive pronoun (mi, ti, si, ci, vi, si) to the subject just like you do with regular verb endings.

25.title: Days of the Week with Definite Articles
25.body: Days of the week don't need articles normally, but adding 'il' or 'la' (for domenica) means 'every' that day.

examples.en: I go to the gym on Monday.
I go to the gym every Monday.

25.tip: Think: no article = specific day; with article = habitual/recurring day.

25.title: Singular vs Plural in Italian
25.body: Italian nouns and adjectives change form based on number. Singular forms follow singular markers like 'uno', while plural forms follow plural markers like 'due'.
25.tip: Think of 'uno' as pointing to one thing, 'due' as pointing to multiple things - the word changes to match.

25.title: Causative Fare with Double Pronouns
25.body: In causative constructions with 'fare', when both an actor and direct object are present, the actor becomes an indirect object pronoun placed before 'fare', and the direct object pronoun attaches to the infinitive.

examples.en: I can let you have them (literally: I can make-to you-them have).
I make him read the book.

25.tip: Remember: Actor becomes indirect (gli/le), object stays direct (lo/la/li/le) and attaches to the infinitive.

25.title: Present Progressive with Stare + -ndo
25.body: To describe actions in progress, use stare + verb ending in -ndo. Form -ndo by dropping -are/-ere/-ire and adding -ando or -endo.
25.tip: Think 'stare + -ing' like English 'to be + -ing'. Watch for irregulars: bere→bevendo, dire→dicendo, fare→facendo.

25.title: Word Stress and Accent Marks
25.body: Words ending in a stressed vowel require an accent mark. For other words, stress follows specific vowel position rules based on the ending.
25.tip: Remember: No accent = stress on last vowel (consonant endings) or third-from-last vowel (-ia/-io endings) or second-from-last vowel (all others).
25.source_label: WORD STRESS AND THE ACCENT MARK

25.title: Singular to Plural Basics
25.body: Most Italian nouns form plurals by changing the final vowel: -o becomes -i, -a becomes -e, -e becomes -i.
25.tip: Think: O→I, A→E, E→I for regular plurals.
25.source_label: FROM SINGULAR TO PLURAL (GENERAL RULES) Examples

25.title: Masculine Plural for Mixed Groups
25.body: When referring to groups of people or animals, use masculine plural if at least one member is male. Use feminine plural only if all members are female.
25.tip: Think 'masculine wins' - one male makes the whole group masculine plural.
25.source_label: MASCULINE PLURAL FOR A MIXED GROUP

25.title: Plural of Invariable Nouns
25.body: Some Italian nouns ending in a stressed vowel or consonant don't change form in the plural.
25.tip: If the word ends with a stressed vowel (à, è, ì, ò, ù) or a consonant, it's probably invariable in plural.
25.source_label: FROM SINGULAR TO PLURAL (GENERAL RULES) Other genderables

25.title: Singular to Plural Endings
25.body: Feminine singular nouns ending in -a change to -e in plural. All other nouns (masculine -o, -e endings) change to -i in plural.
25.tip: Remember: feminine -a becomes -e, everything else becomes -i.

26.title: Ordering with 'Per cominciare'
26.body: Use 'Per cominciare' to introduce your first course when ordering food. It translates to 'To start' or 'For starters'.

examples.en: To start, I would like the pasta.

26.tip: Think of 'cominciare' as 'to commence' or begin your meal.

26.title: Articulated Prepositions with 'di'
26.body: The preposition 'di' combines with definite articles to form articulated prepositions, which contract into single words.
26.tip: Think of 'di' as merging with the article - it always comes first and contracts with what follows.

26.title: Capitalization of Months
26.body: Months of the year in Italian are not capitalized, unlike in English.
26.tip: Think: lowercase for Italian months, uppercase for English months.

26.title: Imperfetto + Past Participle
26.body: Use imperfetto of 'avere' or 'essere' plus past participle to describe ongoing past actions that were interrupted or incomplete.
26.tip: Think 'was/were + -ing' in English but for past actions that were still happening when something else occurred.

26.title: Irregular Past Participles
26.body: Many common Italian verbs have irregular past participles that don't follow the regular -ato, -uto, -ito patterns.
26.tip: These must be memorized individually as they don't follow predictable patterns like regular verbs.

26.title: Present Tense with 'da' for Duration
26.body: Use present tense + 'da' to express how long something has been happening, even when seeing someone again after not seeing them.

examples.en: I have lived here for a year.

examples.it: Non ti vedo da parecchio tempo.
examples.en: I haven't seen you for a long time.

26.tip: Think 'present tense + da + time' = 'have been doing for...' even after a gap.

26.title: Trovare with Venire/Andare a
26.body: When trovare is used with venire a or andare a, it means 'to visit' rather than 'to find'.

examples.it: Perché non ci venite a trovare sabato?
examples.en: Why don't you come visit us on Saturday?

examples.it: Vado a trovare mia nonna domani.
examples.en: I'm going to visit my grandmother tomorrow.

26.tip: Think: 'come to find' = 'come visit' or 'go to find' = 'go visit'.

26.title: Formal vs Informal 'You'
26.body: Use 'Lei' (capitalized) for formal 'you' with third-person singular verbs, while 'tu' uses second-person verbs.
26.tip: Think of formal 'Lei' as 'she' - it uses the same verb forms as 'lei' (she).

26.title: Present Tense Stress Patterns
26.body: Italian verbs follow specific stress patterns in present tense: Class I stresses third vowel from end in singular forms (abito), Class II has variable stress, and Class III mostly follows capisco pattern except about twenty verbs like parto.

examples.en: to live → I live, you live, he/she lives, they live
to win → I win, you win, he/she wins, they win
to leave → I leave, you leave, he/she leaves, they leave

26.tip: Remember: Class I = stress moves back, Class II = watch -cere/-scere/-gere verbs, Class III = mostly -isco except special 20 like partire.

27.title: Past Participle Agreement with 'Essere'
27.body: When forming the passato prossimo with 'essere', the past participle must agree in gender and number with the subject.
27.tip: Think: With 'essere', the ending changes to match the subject (o/a/i/e).

27.title: Formal Imperative with Lei
27.body: Use the third person singular present subjunctive for formal commands when addressing someone as 'Lei'.

examples.en: Tell him (formal) that I'll arrive late.

27.tip: Formal commands = present subjunctive (Lei form) - same as 'che Lei dica/resti' without 'che'.

27.title: Partitive Articles with 'di'
27.body: Use partitive articles (del, dei, dell', degli, della, delle) to express 'some' or 'any' with uncountable nouns. After 'un po' di' (a little), use just 'di' without the article.
27.tip: Think: 'di' alone after 'un po' di', but 'di + article' when saying 'some' directly.

27.title: Verbs of Perception with Infinitives
27.body: Verbs like vedere can take a direct object followed by an infinitive instead of a che clause, creating a more immediate construction.

examples.en: Did you see him get off the bus?

27.tip: Use object + infinitive for direct perception, che + conjugated verb for more descriptive observations.

27.title: Future Tense Irregular Stems
27.body: Future tense endings are always regular (-ò, -ai, -à, -emo, -ete, -anno), but some verbs have irregular stems. Many drop the vowel before -r in the infinitive.
27.tip: Remove the -are/-ere/-ire ending plus the preceding vowel to form many irregular future stems.

27.title: Basic Arithmetic Expressions
27.body: Use 'più' for addition and 'meno' for subtraction, with 'fa' as the equals sign in simple calculations.
27.tip: Think of 'più' as 'plus' and 'meno' as 'minus' - they sound similar to their English equivalents.

27.title: Verbs and -zione Nouns
27.body: Some long Italian verbs stressed on the third-from-last vowel in present singular forms have related nouns ending in -zione, with stress patterns matching English cognates.
27.tip: If the verb stress is on the third vowel from the end (e.g., a-BI-to), its -zione noun often matches English stress (habi-TA-tion).
27.source_label: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN CERTAIN VERBS, WORDS IN -ZIONÉ AND THEIR ENGLISH COGNATES

28.title: Preposition 'a' with Distance/Time
28.body: Use the preposition 'a' before expressions of distance or time when stating how far away something is.

examples.it: Il prossimo è a cinquecento metri.
examples.en: The next one is five hundred meters away.

examples.it: È a dieci minuti da qui.

28.tip: Think 'a' for 'at' a distance/time - similar to English 'at five minutes away'.

28.title: Provare a + infinitive
28.body: The verb 'provare' meaning 'to try' requires the preposition 'a' when followed by an infinitive verb.

examples.it: Ha provato a rimettere in moto?
examples.en: Did he try to start it up again?

28.tip: Think 'provare a' = 'try to' - both have the letter 'a' in the middle.

28.title: Negative Elements After Non
28.body: When using non to negate a verb, other negative words like mai, più, nessuno, niente must come after the verb.
28.tip: Think 'non + verb + negative word' - the extra negative always follows the verb.

28.title: Syllabication Rules
28.body: When breaking words at line ends, follow spoken syllable rules with four exceptions: vowel combinations stay together, tense consonants split between letters, s+consonant stays together, and prefixes may separate.
28.tip: Remember: vowels stick together, double consonants split, s+consonant is a team, and prefixes can detach.

28.title: Plural of nouns ending in -co and -go
28.body: Nouns ending in -co form plurals in -chi if the stress is on the syllable before -co, otherwise in -ci. Nouns ending in -go usually form plurals in -ghi, except for -ologo/-ofago professions which use -gi.
28.tip: Stress before -co? Use -chi. No stress? Use -ci. For -go: professions in -ologo use -gi, others use -ghi.

29.title: Conditional Tense Formation
29.body: The conditional tense uses the same stem as the future tense, with specific endings added to the -r stem.

examples.en: I would do it if I could.

examples.it: Lui ci andrebbe se avesse i soldi.
examples.en: He would go there if he had the money.

29.title: Irregular Conditional Stems
29.body: Three verbs have irregular stems in the conditional: essere → fo-, dare → de-, stare → ste-.

examples.en: I would do it if I were you.

29.tip: Remember FDS: Farei, Darei, Starei for the three irregular conditional stems.

29.title: Pensare + di + Infinitive
29.body: The verb 'pensare' (to think) requires the preposition 'di' when followed by an infinitive verb.

examples.en: We're thinking of keeping it for a week.

29.tip: Think of 'pensare di' as a fixed phrase meaning 'to think about doing' something.

29.title: Guidare vs Andare in macchina
29.body: Use 'guidare' for the action of driving a vehicle. Use 'andare in macchina' to express traveling somewhere by car.

examples.it: Siamo andati in Calabria in macchina.
examples.en: We went to Calabria by car.

29.tip: Guidare = operate the car; Andare in macchina = travel by car

29.title: Reflexive Verb + di + Infinitive
29.body: The reflexive verb raccomandarsi followed by di plus an infinitive expresses strong advice or urging someone to do something.

examples.it: Mi raccomando di tenere le sicure sempre abbassate.
examples.en: I strongly advise you to always keep the safeties on.

29.tip: Think: raccomandarsi di = 'to urge/advise to' - the reflexive form makes it personal advice.

29.title: Apostrophe with elision
29.body: Use an apostrophe when dropping the final vowel of certain words before another word starting with a vowel.
29.tip: Think of the apostrophe as a 'vowel bridge' connecting words that would otherwise clash.

29.title: Gender of Professions
29.body: Some professions have fixed gender regardless of the person's gender, while others change with -a/-o endings.
29.tip: Remember: guida, guardia, spia are always feminine nouns even for males.

29.title: Apocopation
29.body: Apocopation is the dropping of the final unstressed vowel or syllable of a word, often occurring before another word in speech.
29.tip: Think of it as 'breath group' shortening - words often lose their final vowel when flowing into the next word.

29.title: Irregular Gender in Plural Nouns
29.body: Some masculine singular nouns become feminine in the plural, often ending in '-a'. This is a small, irregular group.
29.tip: Think of body parts like 'braccia' (arms) and 'labbra' (lips) as having a feminine plural form.
29.source_label: MASGULINE IN THE SINGULAR AND FEMININE IN THE PLURAL

29.title: Apostrophe in Italian
29.body: An apostrophe replaces a vowel when a word ending in a vowel is followed by another word starting with a vowel.
29.tip: Think of it as vowel collision avoidance - when two vowels meet, the first one gets dropped with an apostrophe.

29.title: Omission of Unit Marker with Essere
29.body: After 'essere', words for nationality, religion, or profession usually omit the unit marker unless modified by an adjective or answering 'Chi è?'
29.tip: No article for simple descriptions, but add 'un/una' when you add an adjective like 'buon' or 'bravo'.
29.source_label: OMISSION AND USE OF THE UNIT MARKER

29.title: Gender Agreement with Extrinsic Nouns
29.body: For animate nouns like 'studente', gender matches the person's sex. For other extrinsic nouns, gender follows either a previously mentioned intrinsic noun or defaults to masculine singular.
29.tip: Animate = match sex; Other = follow previous noun or use masculine.

29.title: Elision Rules for Demonstratives and Adjectives
29.body: Feminine singular forms like 'una' and 'quella' elide before vowel sounds only. Masculine singular forms like 'quel' and 'bel' elide before both vowels and semivowels.
29.tip: Think: Masculine forms are more flexible - they elide before both vowels AND semivowels (like i, u). Feminine forms only elide before pure vowels.

30.title: Negatives in Italian
30.body: In Italian, double negatives are grammatically correct and required to express negation.
30.tip: Think 'non' + negative word = correct negation, unlike English.

30.title: Demonstrative Adjectives
30.body: Use 'questo' for 'this' and 'quello' for 'that'. They must agree in gender and number with the noun they modify.
30.tip: Remember: 'questo' for close things, 'quello' for distant things. Both change endings like regular adjectives.

30.title: Preposizioni Articolate
30.body: Simple prepositions combine with definite articles to form contracted forms. Use these when the preposition is followed by a noun with an article.

examples.en: The book is on the table

30.tip: Remember the pattern: preposition + article = one word (a + il = al, di + la = della, etc.)

30.title: Definite Articles: lo, gli
30.body: Use 'lo' (singular) and 'gli' (plural) before masculine nouns starting with z, or s + consonant.
30.tip: Remember Z and S+C sounds need special articles: lo for one, gli for many.

30.title: Pronomi Personali Diretti
30.body: Direct object pronouns replace nouns that receive the action directly, without prepositions. They come before the verb in most cases.

examples.en: I see Maria → I see her
I call the boys → I call them

30.tip: Remember 'li' for masculine plural, 'le' for feminine singular/plural - they attach directly to the verb.

30.title: Stare + Gerundio
30.body: Use 'stare' + gerund to form the progressive tense, showing ongoing actions in present, past, or future.

examples.en: I was leaving when he called.

30.tip: Think 'to be + -ing' in English, but use 'stare' instead of 'essere'.

30.title: Italian Noun Gender Patterns
30.body: Most nouns ending in -o are masculine and form plurals with -i, while most ending in -a are feminine and form plurals with -e.
30.tip: Think: -o/-i for masculine, -a/-e for feminine (with exceptions like 'mano' which is feminine despite ending in -o).

30.title: Possessive Articles
30.body: Possessive adjectives and pronouns usually require the definite article (il, la, i, le) before them, except with singular kinship terms.
30.tip: Think: 'the my friend' sounds wrong in English but is correct in Italian. Drop 'the' only for close family members.

30.title: Trapassato Prossimo Formation
30.body: Form the trapassato prossimo with the imperfect of 'avere' or 'essere' plus the past participle. Use 'essere' with verbs of motion, state, or reflexive verbs.
30.tip: Think 'had + past participle' in English. Remember: movement/state/reflexive → essere (with gender/number agreement).

30.title: Interrogative Pronouns
30.body: Interrogative pronouns introduce questions and agree in gender and number with the noun they refer to.

examples.en: How many books do you read?

30.tip: Remember: 'quale' is singular, 'quali' is plural; 'quanto/quanta' for singular, 'quanti/quante' for plural.

30.title: Condizionale Passato
30.body: Formed with the conditional of 'avere' or 'essere' plus the past participle. Use 'essere' for movement, state changes, and reflexive verbs.

examples.en: I would have spoken with him.
I would have arrived on time.

30.tip: Remember: AVERE + past participle for most verbs, ESSERE + past participle for movement/change verbs (and match gender/number with ESSERE).

30.title: Adjective Endings by Gender and Number
30.body: Adjectives ending in -o have four forms for masculine/feminine and singular/plural. Adjectives ending in -e have two forms, changing only for plural.

examples.en: tall (masc. sing., fem. sing., masc. pl., fem. pl.)

30.tip: Think: -o adjectives match nouns completely (4 forms), -e adjectives are simpler (2 forms).

30.title: Congiuntivo Passato Formation
30.body: Form the past subjunctive with the present subjunctive of 'avere' or 'essere' plus the past participle. Use 'essere' with verbs of motion, reflexive verbs, and some intransitive verbs.

examples.it: Spero che abbia parlato con lei.
examples.en: I hope he spoke with her.

30.tip: Remember: ESSERE verbs agree in gender/number with the subject (arrivato/a/i/e), while AVERE verbs don't change.

30.title: Futuro Semplice Conjugation
30.body: Form the future tense by adding future endings (-ò, -ai, -à, -emo, -ete, -anno) to the verb's infinitive stem after dropping -are, -ere, or -ire.
30.tip: Future endings are the same for all verb types (-are, -ere, -ire), making this tense easier to conjugate than others.

30.title: Irregular Past Participles
30.body: Some Italian verbs have irregular past participles that don't follow the standard -ato/-uto/-ito patterns and must be memorized.
30.tip: Group irregular participles by pattern: -sto (rimasto), -tto (scritto), -uto (venuto), -so (perso).

30.title: Forming Adverbs with -mente
30.body: Many Italian adverbs are formed by adding -mente to the feminine singular form of the adjective. Adjectives ending in vowel + -le or -re drop the final -e before adding -mente.
30.tip: Think: feminine adjective + mente = adverb. For -le/-re adjectives, just add -mente after removing the final -e.

30.title: Relative Pronouns: che vs. cui
30.body: Use 'che' as subject or direct object of a verb. Use 'cui' after prepositions like di, a, da, in, con, su, per, tra, fra.

examples.it: La persona che sta parlando è americana.
examples.en: The person who is speaking is American.

examples.it: La persona con cui vogliamo parlare è americana.
examples.en: The person with whom we want to speak is American.

30.tip: Remember: CHE = subject/object, CUI = after preposition. Both can be replaced by il/la quale for clarity.

30.title: Present Subjunctive Triggers
30.body: Verbs like 'volere' and 'credere' trigger the present subjunctive in dependent clauses introduced by 'che'.

examples.it: La mamma vuole che faccia un corso d'inglese.
examples.en: Mom wants me to take an English course.
I think it costs about a million.

30.tip: Remember: Wishes, wants, doubts, and opinions (volere, credere, pensare) often need the subjunctive after 'che'.

30.title: Irregular Present Tense Verbs
30.body: Some common verbs like andare, avere, dovere, and dare have irregular conjugations in the present tense that must be memorized.

examples.it: io vado, tu hai, lui dà
examples.en: I go, you have, he gives

examples.it: noi andiamo, voi avete, loro devono
examples.en: we go, you have, they must

30.tip: Remember 'andare' changes stem (vad-/v-/and-), while 'avere' and 'dovere' keep 'h' and 'v' in some forms.

30.title: Passato Prossimo with Essere
30.body: Some verbs use essere instead of avere in passato prossimo. The past participle must agree in gender and number with the subject.
30.tip: Remember verbs of movement, state, or change use essere. The ending changes: -o (masc sing), -a (fem sing), -i (masc plur), -e (fem plur).
30.source_label: PASSATO PROSSIMO (di alcuni verbi usati con essere)

30.title: Passato Prossimo: Avere vs Essere
30.body: Use 'avere' with most verbs (parlato, venduto) and 'essere' with verbs of movement/change (arrivato, caduto). With 'essere', the past participle agrees in gender/number with the subject.
30.tip: ESSERE verbs: Arrive, Leave, Enter, Exit, Fall, Be Born, Die, Stay, Return (ALEES BDSR)

30.title: Italian Verb Conjugations
30.body: Italian verbs are grouped by their infinitive endings: -are (1st conjugation), -ere (2nd conjugation), and -ire (3rd conjugation). Some -ire verbs add -isc- in present tense forms.
30.tip: Remember: ARE = 1st, ERE = 2nd, IRE = 3rd. For -ire verbs, think 'isc' for most, but common ones like 'aprire' skip it.

30.title: Basic Italian Conjunctions
30.body: Conjunctions connect words, phrases, or clauses. Common coordinating conjunctions include 'e' (and), 'o' (or), and 'ma' (but).

examples.en: I see Mario but not Luca.
I will stay home if it rains.

30.tip: Remember E-Ma-O: E (and), Ma (but), O (or) - the three most common coordinating conjunctions.

30.title: Irregular Imperative Forms
30.body: Some verbs have irregular imperative forms that don't follow regular conjugation patterns, especially in the formal 'Lei' and plural 'Loro' forms.
30.tip: Common irregulars: andare → vada/vadano, fare → faccia/facciano, dire → dica/dicano, avere → abbia/abbiano, essere → sia/siano.

30.title: Italian Verb Tense Overview
30.body: Italian verbs conjugate across multiple tenses and moods including indicative, subjunctive, conditional, and imperative forms.

examples.it: Indicativo: parlo (presente), parlerò (futuro), ho parlato (passato prossimo)
examples.en: Indicative: I speak (present), I will speak (future), I have spoken (present perfect)

examples.it: Congiuntivo: che io parli (presente), che io abbia parlato (passato)
examples.en: Subjunctive: that I speak (present), that I have spoken (past)

30.tip: Remember the main categories: Indicative (facts), Subjunctive (doubt/wishes), Conditional (would), Imperative (commands).

30.title: Congiuntivo Trapassato Formation
30.body: Form the past subjunctive with the imperfect subjunctive of 'avere' or 'essere' plus the past participle. Use 'essere' with verbs of motion, reflexive verbs, and verbs indicating change.

examples.it: Speravo che tu avessi parlato con lui.
examples.en: I hoped you had spoken with him.

examples.it: Era importante che loro fossero arrivati in tempo.
examples.en: It was important that they had arrived on time.

30.tip: Think 'had + past participle' in English - same time relationship, just with subjunctive helper verbs.

30.title: Condizionale Presente Conjugation
30.body: Form the present conditional by adding the endings -ei, -esti, -ebbe, -emmo, -este, -ebbero to the verb's infinitive stem (remove -are, -ere, -ire).
30.tip: Think 'would' + verb: take the infinitive, drop the last 'e' (for -are/-ere verbs) and add the conditional endings.

30.title: Congiuntivo Presente - Irregular Verbs
30.body: The present subjunctive has irregular forms for some verbs. For -are verbs like 'parlare', use 'parli-' stem; for -ere verbs like 'vendere', use 'venda-' stem; for -ire verbs like 'capire', use 'capisca-' stem.

examples.it: che io parli, che tu capisca, che noi vendiamo
examples.en: that I speak, that you understand, that we sell

examples.it: che loro arrivino, che voi cadiate
examples.en: that they arrive, that you (plural) fall

30.tip: Remember: -are → parli, -ere → venda, -ire → capisca. The endings are regular once you have the correct stem.

30.title: Imperativo formale e informale
30.body: L'imperativo ha forme diverse per tu/Lei/noi/voi/Loro. Per tu informale usa la forma del presente indicativo, per Lei formale usa la forma del congiuntivo presente.
30.tip: Informale: usa il presente indicativo. Formale: usa il congiuntivo presente (3a persona).

30.title: Congiuntivo Imperfetto Formation
30.body: Form the imperfect subjunctive by taking the infinitive stem, removing -are/-ere/-ire, and adding these endings: -assi, -assi, -asse, -assimo, -aste, -assero for -are verbs; similar patterns for -ere/-ire.

examples.en: to speak → I spoke, you spoke, he/she spoke (subjunctive)
to arrive → I arrived, you arrived, he/she arrived (subjunctive)

30.tip: Think 'imperfect tense' + 'subjunctive mood' = take the infinitive stem and add the special -ss- endings.

30.title: Imperfetto Conjugation Pattern
30.body: The imperfetto tense is formed by removing -are, -ere, or -ire from the infinitive and adding the endings -avo, -avi, -ava, -avamo, -avate, -avano for -are verbs, and -evo, -evi, -eva, -evamo, -evate, -evano for -ere/-ire verbs.
30.tip: Remember: -ARE verbs use -AV- endings, -ERE/-IRE verbs use -EV- endings. All forms have the stress on the vowel before the ending.

30.title: Present Indicative Conjugation Patterns
30.body: Regular verbs in present indicative follow three patterns based on their infinitive endings: -are (parlare), -ere (vendere), and -ire (capire). Each pattern has specific endings for each subject pronoun.

examples.it: io parlo, tu parli, lui parla
examples.en: I speak, you speak, he speaks

examples.it: io vendo, tu vendi, lui vende
examples.en: I sell, you sell, he sells

examples.it: io capisco, tu capisci, lui capisce
examples.en: I understand, you understand, he understands

30.tip: Remember: -are verbs use -o, -i, -a; -ere verbs use -o, -i, -e; -ire verbs add -isc- in three forms (io, tu, lui/lei).

30.title: Essere vs Avere in Compound Tenses
30.body: Most verbs use avere with past participle, but essere is used with verbs of movement, change of state, or reflexive verbs. With essere, the participle agrees in gender/number with the subject.
30.tip: Use essere with verbs that describe coming/going, being born/dying, or entering/exiting. Remember: 'Andare, venire, entrare, uscire' all take essere.

30.title: Chi as Verb Governor
30.body: The word 'chi' (who) always governs verbs in the singular form, even when referring to multiple people.

examples.en: Who is coming to the party?

30.tip: Think of 'chi' as asking about each person individually, so it always takes singular verbs.

30.title: Formal vs Informal Address
30.body: Use 'tu' for informal singular address, 'voi' for informal plural, and 'Lei' for formal singular address regardless of gender.
30.tip: Remember: Lei (formal) uses third person singular verbs like 'parla' instead of 'parli'.
